planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil; sweet autumn clematis establishes quickly and blooms the first year from transplant — keep cool in summer by mulching around the base and providing afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline soil with the crown at or just below the soil surface; sweet autumn clematis explodes into bloom in late summer and fall with masses of small, starry white flowers that cover the vine in a frothy blanket of fragrance reminiscent of vanilla. The plant can grow 15–20 feet in a season once established and needs a strong trellis, arbor, or fence to climb. Prune back hard in late winter (cut to 12–18 inches from the ground) to control size and encourage vigorous new growth, since it blooms on current-season growth. Important note: sweet autumn clematis self-seeds prolifically and is considered invasive in portions of the eastern US; deadhead before seeds mature or remove seedlings promptly to prevent spread into wild areas. Excellent on chain-link fences, pergolas, and as a late-season screen.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks before sowing indoors; plant out after last frost in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil. The plant dies to the ground in winter and re-emerges from the root in spring. Can also be started from softwood cuttings taken in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky or sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas clematis is the only North American clematis species with urn-shaped, non-opening flowers in shades of scarlet to coral-pink — a dramatic departure from the typical flat, open clematis bloom. In its native Texas limestone hill country habitat, it scrambles through shrubs and along rocky slopes. The plant is one of the most drought-tolerant clematis species once established and thrives in the alkaline soils and intense heat of zone 8–9 where many clematis struggle. Its native-species hybrid offspring, including the popular 'Princess Diana' (coral-pink) and 'Duchess of Albany' (pink), are more widely available than the straight species but share its ornamental urn shape. Prune back hard in late winter as the vine blooms on new growth.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; or sow in earliest spring in a shaded, cool, moist seedbed. Herb Robert self-sows prolifically once established and typically perpetuates itself without intervention once a colony is started.

directions: Direct sow or allow to self-sow in moist, shaded, rocky or well-drained soil under trees, along walls, or in shaded garden beds; herb Robert is a dainty annual or biennial with deeply cut, ferny foliage that smells of turpentine when bruised — a scent that may repel insects, which accounts for part of its old folk-medicine reputation as a pest deterrent. The small, bright magenta-pink flowers appear from spring through fall and attract small native bees. In traditional European herbal medicine, the plant was used as an astringent and anti-inflammatory poultice; modern research has found antibacterial compounds in the leaf. In partly shaded rock gardens, walls, and woodland edges, herb Robert forms charming self-sustaining colonies without becoming aggressive. The foliage turns bright red in fall, giving rise to the common name 'red robin.'

planting.note: Sow in midsummer (July to early August) in most climates for fall harvest; in zone 9, sow in August–September for harvest from November through January. Sugarloaf is harvested in fall after cool nights sweeten the heads.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in rows 18 inches apart, thinning to 12 inches when seedlings are established; Sugarloaf or Pan di Zucchero is a distinctive Italian chicory that forms an upright, elongated pale green head resembling a light-green romaine lettuce — quite different in form from the compact red Chioggia or the torpedo-shaped Treviso radicchios. The flavor is characteristically chicory-bitter when young, but sweetens considerably after several frosts or cold nights, becoming almost endive-like in the inner white heart. The heads can be stored after harvest for several weeks in a cool, humid location; the outer leaves are removed and the inner pale heart is eaten fresh in salads or braised. Self-blanching as the tight leaves exclude light from the interior; no additional blanching is required.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3–4 times the bulb diameter (typically 4–6 in) in well-drained soil; bulbs establish roots through winter and send up foliage in early spring with blooms in late spring.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Persian onion produces softball-sized spherical flowerheads in rich violet-purple on straight stems 24–36 inches tall in late spring — a striking vertical accent among lower perennials. The dried seedheads maintain their globe form and are attractive into summer, making them excellent for cutting and drying. Persian onion is an important parent species of the popular garden variety 'Purple Sensation,' which shares its intense color and large globe form. Plant in drifts among roses, peonies, or late-flowering alliums to create a succession of color. The foliage dies back after bloom, so plant among perennials that will mask the yellowing leaves. Reliable and long-lived in zones 4–8 with good drainage; bulbs often naturalize and gradually multiply.
